mission.title: Install and Configure Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS)
mission.description: Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) is a managed Kubernetes service that simplifies the deployment, management, and operations of Kubernetes clusters in Azure. It is ideal for organizations looking to leverage Kubernetes without the overhead of managing the underlying infrastructure.

steps.title: Create an AKS Cluster
steps.description:
Use the Azure CLI to create a new AKS cluster. Replace `<RESOURCE_GROUP>`, `<CLUSTER_NAME>`, and `<LOCATION>` with your desired values. For version 1.31, specify the Kubernetes version explicitly.

```bash
az aks create --resource-group <RESOURCE_GROUP> --name <CLUSTER_NAME> --location <LOCATION> --kubernetes-version 1.31 --node-count 3 --enable-addons monitoring --generate-ssh-keys
```

After the cluster is created, connect to it using kubectl:

```bash
az aks get-credentials --resource-group <RESOURCE_GROUP> --name <CLUSTER_NAME>
```

Verify the connection:

```bash
kubectl cluster-info
kubectl get nodes
```

steps.title: Post-Install Configuration
steps.description:
Enable the Kubernetes Metrics Server for autoscaling. Apply the Metrics Server manifest:

```bash
kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/metrics-server/releases/latest/download/components.yaml
```

Verify that the Metrics Server is running:

```bash
kubectl get deployment metrics-server -n kube-system
```

uninstall.title: Delete the AKS Cluster
uninstall.description:
To remove the AKS cluster and associated resources, use the following command:

```bash
az aks delete --resource-group <RESOURCE_GROUP> --name <CLUSTER_NAME> --yes --no-wait
```

This command will delete the AKS cluster and all associated resources in the specified resource group.

upgrade.title: Upgrade AKS Cluster
upgrade.description:
To upgrade your AKS cluster to a new version, first check the available versions:

```bash
az aks get-upgrades --resource-group <RESOURCE_GROUP> --name <CLUSTER_NAME>
```

Then, upgrade the cluster to the desired version (e.g., 1.31):

```bash
az aks upgrade --resource-group <RESOURCE_GROUP> --name <CLUSTER_NAME> --kubernetes-version 1.31 --yes
```

Before upgrading, ensure you have backups of your workloads and configurations. After the upgrade, verify the cluster status:

```bash
kubectl get nodes
```

troubleshooting.title: Cluster Not Responding
troubleshooting.description:
If your AKS cluster is not responding, check the status of the nodes:

```bash
kubectl get nodes
```

If nodes are in 'NotReady' state, check the events:

```bash
kubectl describe node <NODE_NAME>
```

Common issues include insufficient resources or misconfigured network settings.

troubleshooting.title: Metrics Server Not Working
troubleshooting.description:
If the Metrics Server is not functioning, check its logs:

```bash
kubectl logs -n kube-system deployment/metrics-server
```

Ensure that the Metrics Server is properly deployed and that the API server has the correct flags set for aggregation.

troubleshooting.title: Failed to Connect to AKS
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot connect to your AKS cluster, ensure that you have the correct credentials:

```bash
az aks get-credentials --resource-group <RESOURCE_GROUP> --name <CLUSTER_NAME>
```

Also, verify your Azure subscription and resource group settings.

versionNotes.changes: Introduced support for new Azure regions and improved integration with Azure Monitor.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated support for Kubernetes versions below 1.25.

versionNotes.changes: Enhanced security features and improved scaling capabilities.

resolution.summary: A successful AKS setup includes a running Kubernetes cluster with nodes in 'Ready' state, the Metrics Server deployed, and the ability to scale applications seamlessly. You can verify this by checking the cluster info and node status.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have the Azure CLI installed and configured to access your Azure subscription.
